title: IPO chase and six-month price-action model
question: Do new listings pay off if you buy in the aftermarket, and can that base rate support a reusable six-month IPO scorecard?
finding: Broad aftermarket IPO chasing is a base-rate loser: 760 US IPOs since 2022-10 had a day-1-close one-year median excess return of -28.4% versus SPY, and only 19% beat the index. The six-month model keeps the 180-day -15.4% SPY-excess prior, then adjusts for institutional IPO quality, offering mechanics, underwriters, holder/control risk, market setup, index flow, lock-up supply and business quality. The new RIA equity-supply regime extension adds nuance: IPO waves can be late-cycle warnings, but IPO count alone is not proof of broad-market greed or high multiples. From 1960-2025, IPO count has only a +0.077 Spearman correlation with CAPE, and only 3 of 17 top-quartile IPO-count years were also top-quartile CAPE years. The cleaner lesson is to be more cautious on the freshly issued paper and unlock supply than to mechanically sell the S&P 500.
